items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)
items.title: Diagnosis and Urgency of Treatment in Familial HLH
items.content: Familial Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis (FHL) is a rapidly fatal condition if untreated, with median survival of only 1-2 months. It's crucial to consider FHL when encountering infants with fever and sepsis-like symptoms alongside cytopenias and hepatosplenomegaly, especially if blood cultures are negative.
keyPoints: FHL is rapidly fatal without treatment; median survival is 1–2 months.
Incidence of FHL estimated at 1 in 50,000 live births.
Median age at onset: 3-6 months.
Consider HLH if sepsis-like symptoms with cytopenias and hepatosplenomegaly are present, particularly when blood cultures are negative.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)
items.title: Clinical and Laboratory Features of FHL
items.content: FHL presents with fever, cytopenias (especially thrombocytopenia), hepatosplenomegaly, elevated ferritin, transaminases, and often neurologic symptoms. Hemophagocytosis in bone marrow is a diagnostic criterion.
keyPoints: Common presentation: fever, sepsis-like condition, cytopenias (especially thrombocytopenia), hepatosplenomegaly.
Ferritin and transaminase levels are almost always elevated.
Neurologic symptoms present in about one-third of children at diagnosis.
Hemophagocytosis is a diagnostic criterion.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)
items.title: Pathophysiology and Immune Defects in FHL
items.content: FHL involves defective lymphocyte cytotoxicity, leading to uncontrolled immune activation. Natural killer cells and cytotoxic T cells fail to regulate the immune response, resulting in hypercytokinemia and multiorgan failure.
keyPoints: FHL is characterized by defects in lymphocyte cytotoxicity.
Involves natural killer (NK) cell and cytotoxic T cell dysfunction.
Results in a cytokine storm with elevated levels of IFN-γ, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-18, TNF-α.
Leads to hypercytokinemia and multiorgan failure.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 2) | Possible Primary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis | CLINICAL DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA
items.title: Importance of Early Diagnosis and Treatment in FHL
items.content: Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial for patients with Family Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis (FHL) due to the high risk of rapid death and central nervous system damage if treatment is delayed. Ferritin levels, often elevated in these cases, can serve as a screening test, especially in children.
keyPoints: Early diagnosis prevents rapid death and CNS damage.
Elevated ferritin (>500 μg/L) suggests HLH in children; markedly elevated levels should raise suspicion in adults.
FHL is autosomal recessive with mutations primarily in PRF1 and UNC13D genes.

Five of the following eight criteria (or seven after 2024 changes): fever, splenomegaly, bicytopenia, thrombocytopenia (<100 × 10^9/L), neutropenia (<1.0 × 10^9/L), hypertriglyceridemia (≥3.0 mmol/L), hypofibrinogenemia (≤1.5 g/L), hemoglobinocytosis, ferritin ≥500 μg/L, low or absent NK cell activity, and soluble IL-2 receptor alpha levels ≥2400 U/mL are needed for diagnosis.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 2) | Possible Primary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis | CLINICAL DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA

items.subtopic: FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 2) | Possible Primary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis | CLINICAL DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Genetic Considerations in FHL
items.content: FHL diagnosis can be clinical, genetic, or based on lymphocyte cytotoxicity. Molecular confirmation is the gold standard but may take weeks; thus, the HLH-2004 criteria are used for timely diagnosis. Not all patients meet these criteria at onset, necessitating treatment based on strong clinical suspicion.
keyPoints: FHL caused by autosomal recessive mutations in PRF1, UNC13D, STX11, and STXBP2 genes.
Genetic variants vary by ethnicity; PRF1 and UNC13D are most common.

Diagnosis requires fulfilling five of eight criteria (or seven post-2024 changes) including fever, splenomegaly, bicytopenia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, hypertriglyceridemia, hypofibrinogenemia, hemoglobinocytosis, ferritin ≥500 μg/L, low or absent NK cell activity, and soluble IL-2 receptor alpha levels ≥2400 U/mL.
Therapy may need to start on clinical suspicion due to delayed molecular confirmation.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 2) | Possible Primary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis | CLINICAL DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA

items.subtopic: GENETIC AND CELLULAR DIAGNOSTIC ASSAYS | TREATMENT
items.title: Genetic Confirmation and Initial Management of HLH
items.content: Pathogenic genetic variants are crucial for diagnosing HLH. Functional cytotoxicity assays can suggest genetically determined HLH, with specific assays indicating particular gene variants. At diagnosis, stopping hyperinflammation is critical to patient survival and reducing CNS damage.
keyPoints: Confirmation of pathogenic genetic variants is essential for HLH diagnosis.
Functional assays like absent perforin expression suggest PRF1 variants; defective exocytosis suggests UNC13D, STXBP2, STX11, or RAB27A variants.
Pretransplant therapy aims to stop hyperinflammation and treat triggering factors.
First-line treatments include dexamethasone and etoposide, with pre-HSCT survival almost 90% in experienced centers.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · GENETIC AND CELLULAR DIAGNOSTIC ASSAYS | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: GENETIC AND CELLULAR DIAGNOSTIC ASSAYS | TREATMENT
items.title: Role of HSCT in HLH Treatment
items.content: Allogeneic HSCT is the only curative treatment for FHL. Pretransplant complete remission is beneficial but not mandatory for post-HSCT survival, with survival rates reaching 85-90% in experienced centers.
keyPoints: HSCT is the sole curative option for FHL.
Pretransplant complete remission improves outcomes but isn't required for HSCT success.
Post-HSCT survival is 85–90% in experienced centers.
Overall survival with traditional therapy (etoposide) approaches 80%; nearly 100% in asymptomatic siblings.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · GENETIC AND CELLULAR DIAGNOSTIC ASSAYS | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: OTHER FORMS OF PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Griscelli Syndrome Type 2
items.content: Griscelli syndrome type 2 is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by hypopigmentation and a predisposition to HLH. It involves defects in the RAB27A gene, leading to clinical features similar to familial HLH (FHL). Diagnosis can be aided by observing irregular pigment clumps in hair under light microscopy.
keyPoints: Autosomal recessive disorder
Characterized by hypopigmentation and predisposition to HLH
Caused by RAB27A gene variants
Diagnosis similar to FHL, with pigment clumps visible under light microscopy
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · OTHER FORMS OF PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: OTHER FORMS OF PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.title: Management of X-Linked Lymphoproliferative Syndrome (XLP)
items.content: XLP, with forms XLP1 and XLP2, is associated with increased risk of HLH. XLP1 involves SH2D1A gene variants leading to SAP deficiency, often triggered by EBV infection, where rituximab may be considered. XLP1 patients can have hypogammaglobulinemia treated with immunoglobulin replacement and are at high risk for lymphomas, necessitating early allogeneic HSCT. XLP2 involves XIAP/BIRC4 gene variants, presenting with HLH-like symptoms and inflammatory bowel disease.
keyPoints: XLP1: SH2D1A gene variant, SAP deficiency
EBV infection triggers HLH in XLP1; consider rituximab
XLP1: Hypogammaglobulinemia treatable with immunoglobulin replacement
High risk of lymphomas in XLP1; early allogeneic HSCT recommended
XLP2: XIAP/BIRC4 gene variant, inflammatory bowel disease
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · OTHER FORMS OF PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.title: Distinguishing Primary and Secondary HLH: Implications for Treatment
items.content: Primary HLH typically presents in infancy with a genetic basis, requiring HSCT for cure. In contrast, secondary HLH can occur at any age due to various triggers such as infections or malignancies, often managed without HSCT.
keyPoints: Primary HLH usually requires hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT).
Secondary HLH is associated with triggering factors like infections (50%), neoplasms (48%), autoimmune diseases (13%), and transplantations (4%).
Viral infections, particularly EBV (15%) and HIV (8%), are common triggers in secondary HLH.
Annual incidence of secondary HLH: 4.2 per million people in England; malignancy-associated HLH: 6.2 per million adults in Sweden.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.title: Clinical and Laboratory Features of Secondary HLH
items.content: Patients with secondary HLH present as critically ill, febrile individuals unresponsive to sepsis-directed therapy. Key features include prolonged fever, splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, and elevated inflammatory markers like ferritin and soluble IL-2 receptor alpha.
keyPoints: Prolonged fever is almost always present in secondary HLH.
Splenomegaly and hepatomegaly occur in about two-thirds of patients.
Elevated ferritin and soluble IL-2 receptor alpha are key inflammatory markers.
Thrombocytopenia, anemia, elevated serum transaminases, lactate dehydrogenase, triglycerides, D-dimer, low fibrinogen levels, and disseminated intravascular coagulation are common findings.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.title: Understanding Secondary HLH Pathophysiology and Diagnostic Challenges
items.content: Secondary HLH is driven by multifactorial causes that accumulate to unbalance the immune system, leading to uncontrolled inflammation. Unlike primary HLH, secondary HLH involves reduced numbers of NK cells and cytotoxic T cells, impairing lymphocyte cytotoxic function. Diagnosing secondary HLH is challenging due to its underdiagnosis and lack of specific tests; instead, combinations like HLH-2004 criteria or the HScore are used.
keyPoints: Secondary HLH has multifactorial causes leading to uncontrolled inflammation when a threshold is reached.
Reduced NK cells and cytotoxic T cells impair lymphocyte cytotoxic function in secondary HLH.
No specific diagnostic test for secondary HLH; use combinations like HLH-2004 criteria or HScore.
HLH-2004 criteria: Diagnosis typically based on fulfilling five criteria, but four may suffice in some centers.
The HScore includes variables such as fever, organomegaly, immunosuppression, cytopenias, ferritin, triglycerides, fibrinogen, AST, and hemophagocytosis.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Elevated Ferritin as a Diagnostic Tool in Secondary HLH
items.content: In secondary HLH, elevated ferritin levels can serve as an initial screening test due to their rapid availability. However, the diagnosis often relies on fulfilling clinical and laboratory criteria such as the HLH-2004 or HScore, with caution needed when underlying diseases might fulfill these criteria.
keyPoints: Elevated ferritin levels are a rapid screening tool for secondary HLH.
Diagnosis typically involves fulfilling five of the HLH-2004 criteria, though four may be sufficient in some cases.
The HScore is used to aid diagnosis with variables including fever, organomegaly, immunosuppression, cytopenias, ferritin, triglycerides, fibrinogen, AST, and hemophagocytosis.
Underlying diseases associated with secondary HLH may complicate diagnostic reasoning by fulfilling HLH-2004 criteria.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Viral Triggers and Regional Prevalence in HLH
items.content: Infection-associated hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) is predominantly driven by viral infections, accounting for approximately 70% of cases. Herpes viruses are the leading cause among adults, with Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) being particularly prevalent in children and even more so in East Asia.
keyPoints: Around 70% of infection-associated HLH cases are linked to viral infections.
Herpes viruses account for approximately 60% of adult viral cases; EBV is responsible for about 40%, and cytomegalovirus (CMV) for around 10%.
EBV is the most common viral trigger in children, with a higher frequency observed in East Asia.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INFECTION-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Markers of HLH in Sepsis-like Conditions
items.content: Diagnosing HLH in critically ill patients with sepsis-like symptoms is challenging due to overlapping clinical presentations. Consideration for HLH should arise if there's no response to standard sepsis-directed therapy, supported by specific laboratory findings.
keyPoints: HLH may be triggered by bacterial septicemia but can be difficult to distinguish from sepsis due to similar symptoms.
Consider HLH in patients with a sepsis-like condition unresponsive to adequate sepsis-directed therapy.

Diagnostic support for HLH includes hyperferritinemia, splenomegaly, marked cytopenias, hypofibrinogenemia, hypertriglyceridemia, and distinct cytokine profiles (elevated IFN-γ with moderate IL-6 levels).
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INFECTION-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.title: Approach to Secondary HLH Treatment: Severity-Stratified Therapy
items.content: Treatment of secondary hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) involves identifying and addressing the underlying cause, alongside using HLH-directed therapy tailored to disease severity. For EBV-HLH, a combination of glucocorticoids and etoposide has shown improved survival rates.
keyPoints: Secondary HLH treatment includes finding and treating the underlying cause.
EBV-HLH: Glucocorticoids plus etoposide improves survival; severity-stratified approach recommended.
Mild/moderate EBV-HLH may be treated with glucocorticoids alone or in combination with IVIG/cyclosporin A, escalating to include etoposide if necessary.
Severe EBV-HLH: Suggested treatment includes etoposide, dexamethasone, and rituximab; less intense dosing for secondary HLH compared to primary.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · TREATMENT

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Chronic Active EBV (CAEBV) and Non-EBV Viral HLH
items.content: Chronic active EBV is a progressive condition requiring allogeneic HSCT as the primary treatment, sometimes used as salvage therapy. Non-EBV viral HLH, including HIV-HLH and severe influenza HLH, often requires immunomodulatory agents like glucocorticoids and IVIG.
keyPoints: CAEBV: Highly progressive with organ failure; allogeneic HSCT is recommended.
Non-EBV Viral HLH: Managed by immunomodulatory agents such as glucocorticoids and IVIG.
Severe influenza HLH (e.g., avian influenza A H5N1) requires rapid escalation of treatment.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · TREATMENT

items.title: Management of Infection-Associated HLH: Bacterial, Parasitic, and Fungal Considerations
items.content: In bacterial-associated HLH, M. tuberculosis is common with a 50% mortality rate; treatment includes tuberculostatic drugs combined with glucocorticoids. Leishmania, the most frequent parasite causing HLH, responds well to liposomal amphotericin B. For fungal infections like Histoplasma in immunocompromised patients, amphotericin B is effective.
keyPoints: Bacterial HLH: M. tuberculosis associated with ~50% mortality; treated with tuberculostatic drugs and glucocorticoids.
Parasitic HLH: Leishmania responds to liposomal amphotericin B monotherapy.
Fungal HLH: Histoplasma triggers HLH in immunocompromised individuals, treatable with amphotericin B.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · TREATMENT

items.title: Prognosis and Frequency of Malignancy-Associated HLH
items.content: Malignancy-associated HLH (mal-HLH) is a severe form of secondary HLH with poor prognosis, particularly due to the underlying malignancies. It occurs in about 2.5% of hematologic malignancies in young adult males and has a two-year survival rate of 20-30%. One-month survival during an HLH episode is around 70%, indicating significant mortality risk.
keyPoints: Mal-HLH affects approximately 2.5% of hematologic malignancies in young adult males.
Two-year survival rate for mal-HLH is about 20-30%.
One-month survival during an HLH episode is around 70%.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · MALIGNANCY-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | TREATMENT

items.title: Diagnostic and Predictive Value of IL-2 Receptor Alpha/Ferritin Ratio in Mal-HLH
items.content: The soluble IL-2 receptor alpha/ferritin ratio is a useful diagnostic tool for mal-HLH, particularly lymphoma-associated HLH. A ratio greater than 2.0 has an 85% positive predictive value for identifying lymphoma-associated HLH, while lower ratios are more common in non-mal-HLH cases.
keyPoints: Soluble IL-2 receptor alpha/ferritin ratio >2.0 indicates a high likelihood of lymphoma-associated HLH with an 85% positive predictive value.
Lower ratios are more typical in patients without malignancy-associated HLH.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · MALIGNANCY-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | TREATMENT

items.explanation: Ferritin, an acute-phase reactant, is almost always elevated in HLH.

items.explanation: Griscelli syndrome type 2 is associated with hypopigmentation and immune defects due to mutation in RAB27A.

items.question: What is a diagnostic criterion for familial HLH?
items.explanation: Bicytopenia, affecting at least two of the three lineages (platelets, red cells, white cells), is a diagnostic criterion for HLH.

items.question: Which of the following is a common neurologic symptom in children with familial HLH?
items.explanation: About one third of children with FHL have neurologic symptoms, including ataxia.

items.question: Which gene is most commonly associated with FHL and encodes the protein perforin?
items.explanation: The PRF1 gene is most commonly associated with FHL and encodes the protein perforin.

items.question: What is the significance of elevated ferritin levels in suspected HLH?
options: It confirms the diagnosis of FHL.
It suggests a screening test for every child with suspected HLH.
It rules out other causes of fever.
items.explanation: Elevated ferritin levels are suggested as a screening test in children with suspected HLH.

options: Flow cytometric assay for perforin expression
items.explanation: Absent perforin expression by flow cytometric assay suggests PRF1 genetic variants.

items.question: Which gene variants are indicated by defective patient cell exocytosis assayed by CD107a fluorescence?
items.explanation: Defective patient cell exocytosis suggests UNC13D, STXBP2, STX11, and RAB27A variants.

items.explanation: The HLH-94 and HLH-2004 treatment protocols are based on dexamethasone and etoposide.

items.explanation: Griscelli syndrome type 2 is caused by defect gene variants in RAB27A encoding Rab27a.

items.explanation: Chédiak-Higashi syndrome can be diagnosed by the presence of characteristic giant lysosomes within leukocytes.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · OTHER FORMS OF PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.question: Which treatment is considered for HLH in XLP when triggered by EBV infection?
items.explanation: In XLP, rituximab (anti-CD20) should be considered when HLH is triggered by an Epstein-Barr virus infection.

items.question: Which of the following is a common triggering factor for secondary HLH in adults?
items.explanation: Infections are associated with 50% of HLH cases in adults.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.question: What is the most common type of malignancy associated with secondary HLH?
items.explanation: Hematologic malignancies are the most frequent, especially T- and NK-cell lymphomas.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical clinical feature of secondary HLH?
items.explanation: Rapid weight gain is not mentioned as a typical clinical feature.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.question: Which statement best describes the role of cytokines in secondary HLH?
options: Inflammatory cytokines are not involved in the development of symptoms.
Cytokine patterns differ across various forms and can be used diagnostically.
All forms of secondary HLH have identical cytokine patterns.
Cytokines play no role in either primary or secondary HLH.
items.explanation: The cytokine pattern is different in the various forms of secondary HLH, which can be used diagnostically.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What distinguishes lymphocyte cytotoxicity in familial HLH from that in secondary HLH?
options: Familial HLH is characterized by reduced numbers of cytotoxic cells.
Secondary HLH has normal numbers but defective function of cytotoxic cells.
Both conditions have reduced numbers and defective function of cytotoxic cells.
Secondary HLH often shows reduced numbers of circulating NK and cytotoxic T cells.
items.explanation: In secondary HLH, the number of circulating NK cells and cytotoxic T cells is often reduced, leading to deficient lymphocyte cytotoxic function.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic tool for secondary HLH uses a weighted scoring system based on nine variables?
items.explanation: The HScore is a weighted scoring system based on nine variables used for diagnostic assistance in secondary HLH.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which viral infection is the most common trigger of HLH in children?
items.explanation: EBV is the most common viral trigger of HLH in children.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INFECTION-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.question: What percentage of secondary HLH cases are associated with bacterial infections?
items.explanation: Around 20% of secondary HLH cases are associated with bacterial infections.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INFECTION-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.question: Which cytokine is markedly elevated in HLH but only moderately elevated in sepsis?
items.explanation: IFN-gamma is markedly elevated in HLH, whereas it is only moderately elevated in sepsis.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INFECTION-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.question: Which combination of drugs was shown to markedly improve survival in secondary HLH, particularly EBV-HLH?
options: IL-1 inhibitors and Janus kinase inhibitors
items.explanation: Studies in Japan showed that a combination of glucocorticoids and etoposide improved survival markedly in secondary HLH.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · TREATMENT

items.question: What is the recommended treatment for chronic active EBV (CAEBV)?
items.explanation: Allogeneic HSCT is the recommended treatment for chronic active EBV (CAEBV).
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · TREATMENT

items.question: In non-EBV viral HLH, which agents are typically used to manage mild to moderate cases?
items.explanation: Non-EBV viral HLH can be managed by immunomodulatory agents such as glucocorticoids and IVIG.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · TREATMENT

items.question: Which drug is recommended for monotherapy in Leishmania-associated HLH?
items.explanation: Liposomal amphotericin B monotherapy is recommended and effective for Leishmania-associated HLH.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · TREATMENT

items.question: Which malignancy is most frequently associated with malignancy-associated HLH (mal-HLH)?
items.explanation: Lymphomas are found in about half of all cases of mal-HLH.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · MALIGNANCY-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | TREATMENT

items.question: What is the reported one-month survival rate for patients with malignancy-associated HLH, primarily representing the HLH episode?
items.explanation: The one-month survival rate has been reported to be around 70%.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · MALIGNANCY-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | TREATMENT

items.question: What is the positive predictive value of a soluble IL-2 receptor alpha/ferritin ratio >2.0 for lymphoma-associated HLH?
items.explanation: A soluble IL-2 receptor alpha/ferritin ratio >2.0 has a positive predictive value of 85% for lymphoma-associated HLH.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · MALIGNANCY-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)
items.statement: Familial HLH is more common in males than females.
items.explanation: FHL is equally common in males and females due to its autosomal recessive inheritance.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)
items.statement: Prompt treatment of HLH can reduce the risk of neurologic sequelae.
items.explanation: Prompt treatment is essential to reduce long-term complications such as neurologic sequelae.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 2) | Possible Primary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytos
items.statement: The diagnosis of FHL can be made solely by evaluating lymphocyte cytotoxicity.
items.explanation: FHL diagnosis is based on clinical, genetic, and evaluation of lymphocyte cytotoxicity; molecular confirmation is the gold standard.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 2) | Possible Primary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis | CLINICAL DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA

items.subtopic: FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 2) | Possible Primary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytos
items.statement: The HLH-2004 criteria require five out of eight specific criteria to diagnose FHL.
items.explanation: Five of the eight specified criteria are needed for an FHL diagnosis according to the HLH-2004 criteria.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 2) | Possible Primary Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis | CLINICAL DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA

items.subtopic: GENETIC AND CELLULAR DIAGNOSTIC ASSAYS | TREATMENT
items.statement: Pretransplant complete remission is mandatory to achieve post-HSCT survival.
items.explanation: Pretransplant complete remission is beneficial but not mandatory for post-HSCT survival.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · GENETIC AND CELLULAR DIAGNOSTIC ASSAYS | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: GENETIC AND CELLULAR DIAGNOSTIC ASSAYS | TREATMENT
items.statement: Emapalumab alone appears to be highly effective in patients with verified FHL.
items.explanation: Emapalumab (anti-IFN-$ \gamma $) alone is less effective in these patients.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · GENETIC AND CELLULAR DIAGNOSTIC ASSAYS | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: OTHER FORMS OF PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.statement: Allogeneic HSCT cures the neurologic disorder in Chédiak-Higashi syndrome.
items.explanation: Allogeneic HSCT does not cure the progressive neurologic disorder associated with Chédiak-Higashi syndrome.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · OTHER FORMS OF PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: OTHER FORMS OF PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.statement: XLP1 is associated with a dysgammaglobulinemia, typically hypogammaglobulinemia of one or more immunoglobulin subclasses.
items.explanation: XLP1 is indeed associated with dysgammaglobulinemia, often presenting as hypogammaglobulinemia.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · OTHER FORMS OF PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.statement: Hemophagocytosis must be present for the diagnosis of secondary HLH.
items.explanation: Hemophagocytosis is not always detected and is not a prerequisite for diagnosis.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.statement: Secondary HLH can be triggered by autoimmune diseases.
items.explanation: Autoimmune diseases are among the triggering factors, with systemic lupus erythematosus being common.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INTRODUCTION | CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FEATURES

items.statement: Secondary HLH is an inherited disorder.
items.explanation: Secondary HLH is not an inherited disorder, though genetic variants may be present.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Elevated ferritin levels can serve as a rapid screening test for secondary HLH.
items.explanation: Elevated ferritin levels may serve as a rapid and readily available screening test for secondary HLH.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Herpes viruses account for approximately 60% of reported viral cases in adults.
items.explanation: Herpes viruses are responsible for around 60% of the viral cases in adults.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INFECTION-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.statement: HLH is most often caused by fungal infections.
items.explanation: Fungal HLH accounts for only 10% of cases, with viral infections being the most common.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · INFECTION-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.statement: Etoposide treatment in secondary HLH should be as intense as in primary HLH.
items.explanation: In secondary HLH, etoposide treatment is suggested to be less intense than in primary HLH.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · TREATMENT

items.statement: Most cases of non-EBV viral HLH are severe and require intensive therapy.
items.explanation: Most cases of non-EBV viral HLH are mild to moderate and can be managed by immunomodulatory agents alone.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · TREATMENT

items.statement: The prognosis of malignancy-associated HLH is generally better than that of infection-associated HLH.
items.explanation: Mal-HLH has the worst prognosis among forms of secondary HLH.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · MALIGNANCY-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | TREATMENT

items.statement: Repeated bone marrow examinations and biopsies may be required if the cause of secondary HLH is not identified.
items.explanation: Searching repeatedly for an underlying (occult) malignancy is prudent if one does not find the cause.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · MALIGNANCY-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)
items.assertion: Ferritin levels are specific for diagnosing HLH.
items.reason: Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant and a marker of inflammation, but not specific to HLH.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because ferritin is not specific to HLH; it serves as a rapid screening test.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)
items.assertion: Defective function of natural killer cells and cytotoxic T cells leads to uncontrolled immune response in FHL.
items.reason: Cytotoxic lymphocytes secrete granules containing perforin and granzymes, which are crucial for apoptosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why defective cytotoxic function leads to uncontrolled immune response.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | FAMILY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER FORMS OF PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS
items.assertion: XLP2 is also called XIAP deficiency.
items.reason: The disease course in XLP2 can be variable and sometimes controlled by mild immunosuppression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion that XLP2 is called XIAP deficiency and the reason about its variable disease course are true, but the latter does not explain the former.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · OTHER FORMS OF PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS

items.assertion: The threshold model explains the uncontrolled inflammation in secondary HLH.
items.reason: Factors triggering and unbalancing the immune system accumulate, eventually reaching a threshold at which inflammation becomes uncontrolled.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Allogeneic HSCT is recommended for chronic active EBV (CAEBV).
items.reason: Chronic active EBV (CAEBV) is a highly progressive disease with organ failure and severe hypercytokinemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why allogeneic HSCT is recommended for CAEBV.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · TREATMENT

items.assertion: M. tuberculosis-associated HLH has a low mortality rate.
items.reason: Tuberculostatic drugs in combination with mild immunomodulatory therapy may be effective.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the mortality rate for M. tuberculosis-associated HLH is approximately 50%.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · TREATMENT

items.assertion: Mal-HLH has a worse prognosis than other forms of secondary HLH.
items.reason: The underlying malignancies associated with mal-HLH contribute to poor survival rates.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: PRIMARY HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS · MALIGNANCY-ASSOCIATED HEMOPHAGOCYTIC LYMPHOHISTIOCYTOSIS | TREATMENT
